trainees.name: Michael Wilson
trainees.current_summary: Senior trainee acting as a de facto coach, and the gym's model for self-driven ringwork. June 2 was a short night cut off when the ring broke: he did well on back rolls, then he and Brandon were trying a new leg-drop move when the ring gave out and training paused for promos; earlier he was sent to work lockups with the new trainees Jacob and Damien. May 26 was a clean bumping session — good snap bumps with no notes, no feedback needed on either shoulder-tackle or back-elbow rounds, and in Cade's heat drill he was made the explicit example for the whole group: always getting back up and moving on his own without relying on Cade to prompt him, with Cade telling the others they should all be doing the same. May 19 fit the pattern: Jimmy used him (with guest trainer Cade Powers) to demonstrate correct suplex form for the group, and he gave Dalton a body slam with no notes on his execution. He also wrestled the night's standout practice match against Tyler — chained lockup/wrist-control reversals into a short chain-wrestling spot, a believable failed-shoulder-tackle sequence built around his size, selling a suplex and a knee before firing back with punches, and eating the finishing body slam. Trainers called it more technical refinement than correction; Jimmy's only note to the pair was to re-attempt shoulder tackles immediately off the ropes rather than circling. On May 15 served as the drop-toe-hold giver for the whole face-bump block and coached Jack through his first take. Continues to draw zero flags on his own work.

trainees.name: Hailey Stokely
trainees.current_summary: Veteran trainee operating at near-trainer level. June 2 fit the pattern: great back rolls, an impromptu rope-running and dropdown drill with guest trainer Kytel, helped break in the new trainees by pairing with Jacob Renzo on lockups, and capped the night cutting a great promo with Jimmy. May 26 was effortless: clean snap bumps with no feedback needed across both the clothesline and back-elbow rounds, and on shoulder tackles she did well and even requested an extra attempt to refine the technique on her own initiative. Sailed through the heat drill (did very well). The kind of session where she draws zero correction and quietly sets the standard. May 19 underscored it: took the suplex flawlessly and the body slam without issue, then Jimmy used her twice as the demonstration model — first for stiff 'board' body-slam form for the group, then to show that size doesn't matter when giving a body slam (she gave guest trainer Kytel a clean body slam after some form coaching). Capped the night with a polished heel performance in a five-minute match against Nick: stalled the start ('match starts when I'm ready'), worked the hand-switch fake and a foot stomp, ran wrist-lock-to-headlock and a shoulder tackle, then sold a hope-spot loss — Nick caught her with drop-downs, three clotheslines, and a roll-up for the finish. Not at May 15. May 12 she effectively co-ran the spot-one block — coached Jack through every unfamiliar piece (lockup-to-headlock transition, leapfrog form including where to bend and how low, hip toss form including foot placement and jump mechanics), then drilled the jumper role on the leapfrog with him (her first time as jumper) until she felt comfortable. Hit spot one flawlessly at full speed with Jack, then volunteered to run it a second time with Jeff to balance the odd numbers — also clean. Ran the second-rope hurricanrana on Jimmy on the turnbuckle and worked satisfaction with Michael. One self-inflicted clip on her first frog splash off the second rope — hand placement was slightly off. Caught DC's head dropping during the headlock, and shut down his promo cleanly with Jimmy when he crossed a personal-insult line. Currently locking in the hurricanrana and a satisfaction ahead of the next TAW show.

trainees.name: Jacob Justice
trainees.notes: Marked inactive May 12, 2026 after six consecutive absences from April 7 onward. Not expected to return to training.
trainees.current_summary: Selling visibly improved through early spring, but he hasn't been at a session since April 7 — six consecutive absences and counting. Confidence and pacing were the biggest gaps when he was active: flagged as timid and slow on rope-throw drills on April 7, and flagged as an injury risk for landing elbow-first on bumps. Hard to track progress with this much absence.

trainees.name: Tyler Skelton
trainees.current_summary: Big-frame trainee still learning how his height and size shape his ringwork. May 19 was a strong outing: brief hesitation on the suplex then took it cleanly, and stepped in to learn how to GIVE a body slam, executing it just fine. He also worked the night's standout five-minute practice match against Michael — chain-wrestling reversals, a failed-shoulder-tackle sequence playing off Michael's size, a corner splash, a suplex, and a multi-step setup into the finishing body slam. Trainers framed their feedback as refinement (Jimmy: re-attempt shoulder tackles off the ropes; Cody: volume, selling, pacing) rather than correction. May 15 had a clean lockup with Jeff (no notes) and a believable one with Michael, plus a clean run as the drop-toe-hold giver with Brandon after some execution pointers from Jimmy on the second attempt. The flag from this session was on the face-bump receiving end — he was using his hands too much to catch himself during the drop-toe-hold face bump, and Joey read it as mostly psychological. Jimmy's face-bump spot-check after the initial drills was clean for him. May 8: handled front rolls cleanly after Cody form coaching, took the kick + back forearm with no notes. German suplex came together cleanly on April 24. Not at May 12.

trainees.name: Jay Taylor
trainees.notes: Removed from program April 14, 2026. Blackballed from local wrestling scene due to unsafe work, lack of attendance, refusal to accept criticism.

trainees.name: DC
trainees.notes: Removed from the training program May 15, 2026 by unanimous Wrestler's Court vote following the May 12 promo incident (personal insults toward Hailey). Jimmy, Cody, and Kytel determined Wrestler's Court was the most impartial way to handle the decision. DC pled his case in front of the trainees; each trainee voted in the locker room with reasoning. DC's defense involved disturbing allegations — locker-room vote notes kept separately at Joey's call, not in this record. Prior conduct flag: May 12 promo, called Hailey a bitch, crossed other personal lines.
trainees.current_summary: Removed from the program by unanimous Wrestler's Court vote on May 15, 2026. He sat out the front of the May 15 session in a meeting with Jimmy, Cody, and Kytel about the May 12 promo incident, then pled his case to the trainees before the vote. Technical record before removal: form fundamentals still needed polish at the end — stiff lockups, head dropping during the headlock, drifting on the two-person rope-crossing drill, timing/coordination off on his first spot-one attempt on May 12. The decisive issue was conduct, not technique: in promos opposite Hailey on May 12 he tried to make her go first 'because she's a woman' despite her being the heel, then in his own promo got too heated and called her a bitch among other personal lines. Jimmy and Hailey stopped the promo on the spot and made clear that personal insults were a hard line; Wrestler's Court three days later closed the program out.

trainees.name: Dalton
trainees.current_summary: The most coaching-intensive trainee in the program. June 2 showed the familiar pattern: he struggled with back rolls — more often doing back bumps instead of rolls — but after one-on-one coaching from Jimmy he landed an acceptable roll, and he did well on the bumps in the bump-and-feed drill with a note to feed up a little faster. May 26 showed real signs of it clicking. He was the very first up on snap bumps and bumped too early — the session's teaching moment for 'shadow bumping' — but cleaned it up on attempt 2, and Cade told him he did well on round 2. The recurring theme resurfaced on shoulder tackles: Jimmy called him out for not selling and stressed building the habit so no-selling never becomes a show problem; he sold the very next rep much better. From there he was solid — did well on back elbows, and turned in the strongest heat-drill performance of anyone, drawing positive feedback all around with no notes. Absorbs correction fast and is starting to string together clean, well-sold work. May 19 stayed true to form. On the suplex he went over but didn't get enough jump, so the landing was awkward — Cade Powers had him drill the jump in suplex position a few reps until he could take it cleanly. On flip bumps Jimmy flagged him for dropping his shoulder too much; after coaching he improved, Jimmy demonstrated a couple as reference, and his later reps looked good. Receives a lot of correction but absorbs it within the session. May 15 fit that pattern almost end to end — corrected on hand placement during his lockup with Tyler, multiple corrections on face-bump form during drop toe holds with Michael, flagged for crooked running (Michael and Brent demonstrated correct form before a better third attempt), then drilled turnbuckle running for refinement after coaching on how to sell being thrown into the corner. On the trading drop-toe-hold drill he got leg-placement pointers as the giver with Nick, much better on the second attempt, and ran a couple of clean reps giving to Kirkland after. **The bright spot of his session: when Jimmy came out to spot-check face bumps, Dalton got an explicit positive flag for good execution** — first piece of unprompted praise from Jimmy in his record. No notes on hip tosses after Brent's walk-through. Returned after missing May 8 and May 12.

trainees.name: Jack
trainees.notes: Age 14. Non-bumping restriction lifted May 5, 2026 — parent always present at training.
trainees.current_summary: Ten-plus weeks in and still on a fast track. June 2 added a new first: he'd never done back rolls before, got a coaching pass from Jimmy, then executed two great back rolls — the pattern of brief coaching followed by clean execution holds. Across the bump-on-contact drill he did well but was noted twice for bumping a bit early. Cut a short but good promo with Dalton. May 26 was a tidy bumping session: a timing note from Cade on his first snap bump, then he did well across the shoulder-tackle round with a coaching point on not getting crooked, and did well on back elbows with notes on selling. His standout was the heat drill — remarkable selling with no notes or negatives at all, including the choke/eye-poke selling pointers the trainers covered. Continues the pattern of brief coaching followed by strong execution. May 19 produced the session's standout moment: Jack didn't feel comfortable executing the suplex at first and opted to wait, so the crash pad was brought out to soften the bump and remove the psychological block — he then took it flawlessly, asked to go again (second clean rep), had the pad removed, and took the suplex fine without it. He rode that confidence the rest of the night. Also first to volunteer for the body slam (clean take), and on flip bumps had a good first rep (could get a touch more height) with much-improved reps after a 'straighten up' note. The crash-pad breakthrough is a textbook example of how to coach him through hesitation. May 15 was a heavy first-time session: first time on the headlock-to-wrist-control drill (Brent walked him slowly through it, then he picked it up quickly), first time taking a drop toe hold from Michael (clean after the coaching pass), first time giving a drop toe hold to Nick (clean on the second attempt with refinement reps that all looked good). Got a hesitation note on his first hip toss, much better on the next rep. Kirkland coached him on foot placement in his lockup with Brent — after the note everything looked great — and Brent flagged his lockup with Nick as looking really good. Got a leg-placement coaching point on his initial face bump too. May 12 was first time on lockup-to-headlock transition, first time as the jumper's base on a leapfrog, first time as the hip-toss thrower, first promo. Pattern holds: brief coaching, then clean execution.

trainees.name: Nick
trainees.notes: Jack's dad. Officially joined the program April 14, 2026. Left the program June 2, 2026 after disclosing pre-existing back and nerve problems — decided he didn't want wrestling to exacerbate them. History preserved.
trainees.current_summary: Left the training program on June 2, 2026 after disclosing pre-existing back and nerve problems, concluding he didn't want wrestling to worsen them. His final logged session (May 26) was a safety setback that may have factored in: on his first snap bump he ghost bumped, landed wrong, and hurt his back, sitting out the rest of the night. Eight sessions in. May 26 was a setback on the safety front: on his first snap bump he ghost bumped (bumped too early), landed wrong, and hurt his back — he was asked not to bump for the rest of the session and sat out the shoulder-tackle, back-elbow, and heat-drill rounds. Worth watching that the early-bump habit doesn't recur, since it's now caused an injury. Prior to this he'd been the easiest trainee in the gym to coach. May 19 he took a body slam with a refinement note on staying stiff and not sandbagging, then took another from guest trainer Kytel and executed it flawlessly; as a body-slam giver he needed to mind foot placement but it went well overall. Notably, he held his own in a full five-minute practice match as the babyface against heel Hailey — selling her control segment (leg stomps, the foot slam) and then hitting the comeback with drop-downs, three clotheslines, and a roll-up for the win. Real growth from rep work into match structure. Six sessions in and still the easiest trainee in the gym to coach. May 15 went almost note-free across every block: Brent coached him and Jeff on looking strong in the lockup-to-corner push and both looked good after; clean run through the headlock drill; no notes on the initial face bump; took the drop toe hold from Michael clean after a coaching pass; volunteered to take from Jeff (Jeff's first time giving) and Jeff did well on it; gave a drop toe hold himself with no execution issues; clean as the hip-toss recipient. The May 12 spot-one timing/coordination issues paired with DC were not specifically called out as Nick's. Continues to absorb corrections on the first rep.

trainees.name: Brandon
trainees.notes: Joined the program May 5, 2026 from Cody McCulley's personal roster. Has been training for a while and has worked a few matches at TAW.
trainees.current_summary: Five sessions in. June 2: good back rolls, paired with new trainee Damien to help teach lockups, and was attempting a new leg-drop move with Michael when the ring broke. His promo with Jimmy started well but fell flat at the end when he ran out of things to say — a stamina/material gap to work on. May 26 was a strong bumping session — on snap bumps his second rep was a touch slow, which he self-corrected by calling it out himself after getting up (good self-awareness), and rounds 2 needed no feedback. On shoulder tackles he did well and Cade had him stick for a beat after the bump for selling; on back elbows he got a form note. Capped it with a very strong heat drill, doing well throughout. His TAW match experience continues to show in how cleanly he absorbs new material. May 15 was almost entirely clean — Kirkland coached him on making the lockup struggle look realistic in either direction (one of his first lockups), and from there everything ran clean: clean drop-toe-hold form and execution taking from Michael, clean face bump in Jimmy's spot-check, clean as the drop-toe-hold giver with Jeff. No notes on the headlock drill from Brent. May 12 was his first time on spot one — Michael walked him through the leapfrog and up-and-over and Jimmy commended him directly. The earlier May 12 form flags (tailbone landing on bumps, drifting on the rope-crossing drill) did not repeat on May 15. Joining from Cody's roster with TAW match experience tracks with how quickly he absorbs new spots.

trainees.name: Jeff
trainees.notes: Joined the program May 5, 2026 from Cody McCulley's personal roster. Relatively new to training.
trainees.current_summary: Seven sessions in. June 2 was a clean, low-key night: did well on his back rolls and did well on the bump-and-feed drill with no flags. May 26 showed clear forward motion on the bumping fundamentals that have been his growth edge. On snap bumps he did well, with the useful coaching point that a snap bump being fast doesn't mean everything else has to speed up. On shoulder tackles he did well but got notes to float less / cover less distance, and to bump less crooked and sell better — then on the back-elbow round Cade told him he took the best bump Cade has seen him take, a real milestone. Solid heat drill to close. Crookedness and selling are still the things to keep tightening, but the trajectory is up. May 19 was a developmental session focused on his bumping fundamentals. On the drop-down drill he started getting up a touch early on his first rep, took light correction, then was much better. On the suplex, Cody worked the form and sequence with him first, but he didn't have enough jump height — the clear takeaway was that Jeff should refine flip bumps before re-attempting the suplex. On flip bumps he initially used his hands to support himself upside down; he worked onto the crash pad and got useful cues (throw the arms down for momentum, come in tall before initiating the flip rather than leaning into it), with form noted as improving by the end. Bumping mechanics are his current growth edge. May 15 was almost note-free in the early going: Brent coached him and Nick on looking strong in the lockup-to-corner push, both looked good after; clean run through the headlock drill (no notes); good first drop-toe-hold attempt with just a small forearm-placement note; gave drop toe holds to Nick (Nick volunteered to take from him) and did well. The one real flag of the session was on hip tosses — he flipped early on his first attempt, then subsequent attempts came together after posting and placement coaching. May 12: clean run through spot one with Hailey (his first time on the sequence) and first-ever promo paired with Brandon, with Jimmy stepping in to demonstrate. Picks up corrections fast on the next rep.

trainees.name: Damien
trainees.current_summary: First session June 2, 2026. Opened on back rolls and did well for a first-timer; some form issues on his second round (expected this early), corrected quickly after light coaching from Jimmy and looked much better. Paired with Brandon to learn lockups. Part of Jimmy's wrestling-psychology discussion, who asked the new trainees about their goals and what brought them to wrestling.

trainees.name: Jacob Renzo
trainees.notes: New trainee, joined June 2, 2026. Distinct from former trainee Jacob Justice (inactive).
trainees.current_summary: First session June 2, 2026. Did well on his first back rolls. Paired with Hailey to learn lockups, and cut a good promo opposite guest trainer Kytel. Part of Jimmy's wrestling-psychology discussion, who asked the new trainees about their goals and what brought them to wrestling.
